title: India-Canada CEPA Negotiations Launch: Economic Reset After Diplomatic Rupture
lede: India and Canada have formalized Terms of Reference for a Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement targeting USD 50 billion in bilateral trade by 2030, marking an economic recalibration following years of diplomatic tension.

sections.context: The launch of CEPA negotiations during Canadian Prime Minister Carney's February 27–March 2, 2026 visit to India represents a deliberate pivot toward economic engagement despite the fraught diplomatic history between the two nations. The Terms of Reference framework establishes the structure, frequency, and modalities for negotiations covering trade in goods, services, and agreed policy areas—positioning the agreement as both ambitious and mutually beneficial. The explicit trade target of USD 50 billion by 2030 signals New Delhi and Ottawa's commitment to scaling bilateral commerce as a hedge against political volatility. This economic repositioning comes as the Carney administration appears intent on recalibrating Canada's approach to India after a period of acute bilateral dysfunction.
sections.threads: The CEPA launch sits alongside parallel frameworks reflecting broader India-Canada institutional renewal. A trilateral MoU involving India, Canada, and Australia on technology and innovation (the ACITI Partnership) underscores New Delhi's willingness to integrate Canadian partners into strategic technology cooperation—a domain traditionally reserved for trusted democratic allies. Additionally, a Declaration of Intent on a Joint Pulse Protein Centre of Excellence demonstrates sectoral specificity in partnership architecture, suggesting both capitals are moving beyond diplomatic rhetoric to concrete institutional mechanisms. These layered agreements echo the strategic partnership framework being formalized under the 'One Earth' platform, indicating a comprehensive reset across multiple bilateral tracks rather than isolated economic gestures.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor three decision points over the next 18 months: (1) the pace and substance of CEPA chapter negotiations, particularly on goods tariffs and rules of origin, which will test whether both sides prioritize trade volumes over sectoral protections; (2) the operationalization of the ACITI Partnership and whether it acquires meaningful technology transfer commitments or remains aspirational; and (3) whether the USD 50 billion target becomes a political metric driving or constraining negotiation outcomes. The silent variable is domestic political durability—whether new Canadian administrations will sustain commitments made under Carney's tenure, given the historical pattern of diplomatic rupture and reset cycles. Watch for early signals on agricultural market access and mobility provisions, both historically contentious domains in India-Canada negotiations.
